lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in, folks, and the numbers are already running hot. I'm seeing East Side activity that would've been a full night's work in 2165. It's 21:00 and the city's already bleeding.

You know what I love about this job? I get to sit in a concrete box, pour whiskey that costs more than it should, and watch Madison kill itself in real-time. It's like being the house band at the end of the world.

I'm pouring number three right now. You can hear it. That sound — that's the sound of a man who used to sleep in the Capitol building, and now he broadcasts from a bunker on Purge Night, and somehow that's not the weirdest career trajectory in this city.

The Vance administration — God rest their authoritarian souls — they really thought this would work. They really thought you could just tell people, 'Hey, one night a year, no rules, go crazy,' and then everyone would shake hands on the 8th and go back to work.
What they didn't factor in: people don't forget. You give someone that kind of permission once, they spend 364 days remembering what it felt like to not ask for permission.
Caller: Marcus from Atwood — go ahead, Marcus.
Marcus, I'm going to level with you: if your neighbor's already looking at you different, there's not much I can say that'll change it. What's done is done.
No, I'm not saying — I'm saying the Purge doesn't change people. It reveals them. You already knew he was angry. Tonight he just stopped hiding it.
Stay inside, Marcus. Lock the door. Call back at 6am and we'll talk about what you do on the 24th.
See, that's the thing nobody wants to talk about. The Purge ends at 7am, and then what? Everyone pretends? Marcus has to see his neighbor at the grocery store in four weeks.

The Vance crowd — and I use the past tense with extreme confidence — they thought this would be cathartic. Blow off steam, come back reset. What they got was a city that knows exactly what its neighbors are capable of.

I had a colleague in the 2040s — Director Hegseth's people, actually — who said the Purge was 'preventative medicine for the national psyche.' That man is not with us anymore. I don't know if he made it past his first Purge or if he's just retired somewhere in Canada, but the phrase stuck with me.

Preventative medicine. Like we're all walking around with a tumor and one night a year we get to let it out, and somehow that makes us healthier. That's not medicine. That's a pressure valve on a bomb.
And we're only two hours in.
I'm pouring again. This is number four. I know because Tim keeps looking at me — very subtly, very Tim — and doing the math in his head about how many more hours he has to cover me.
Tim, I can feel the judgment from the booth. I'm fine. I'm a professional. I'm a drunk, and a professional, and I contain multitudes.
Caller: Jennifer from State Street — Jennifer, are you safe?
Jen, I need you to listen to me very carefully: if you're in the ruins, you need to move now. Not later. Not after you lock the windows. Now.
I don't care about your apartment. I don't care about your stuff. State Street at 21:00 on Purge Night is not a place to be sentimental.
Yes, I know you've always been fine before. Yes, I know you've stayed home every other year. This year is different. This year the numbers came in early and they came in ugly.
Get to the Capitol area. Find a barricade. Willy Street if you can make it. Anywhere but State Street after 22:00.
No, I'm not trying to scare you. I'm trying to keep you from becoming a data point for Tucker to report at 23:55.
Okay. Okay, Jen. Keep the line open to your phone. Tim's got your location. We're logging this. You get somewhere safe, you text the switchboard. Don't call back — just text.
Stay low. Stay quiet. And Jen — seriously — get out of State Street.
She's hanging up. Tim's got her. Let me know if she makes it, Tim. I'll talk about it at 4am when I'm too drunk to care if I'm being unprofessional.
That's the thing about this gig. You can't unsee it. Jennifer's leaving her apartment right now, or she's not, and either way I just accused her of being sentimental about her own life.
There's a whiskey in front of me and it's not going anywhere. I might as well commit.

You know who would've loved this? RFK. Not the real RFK — the one from the history books. The Vance-era health guy, the one who thought the answer to every social problem was 'free will' and 'personal choice.'

He's dead. Died 2089. I don't know of what. Nobody talks about it. But if he were alive, he'd probably be somewhere watching Purge Night go out live and thinking, 'Yes, this is what freedom looks like.'
Freedom. I used to know what that word meant. Now it just means twelve hours of not calling the cops.
Caller: David from Vilas Park — David, what's on your mind?
David, that's a very specific question. You're asking me if 187 from last year means the year is getting worse or better. I'll be honest with you: it means nothing good.

Yeah, it's down from 201 in 2164. But it's up from 179 in 2167. What you're seeing is trend stability at a very high number, which is just a polite way of saying we've normalized mass casualty events.

The Hegseth Doctrine — you remember that? 'Some acceptable losses.' That was the phrase. And every year since, we've calculated what acceptable means, and the number is always higher than you'd think.
Last year we lost 187 people in this city in twelve hours. You know how many of them had names that appeared in the newspaper? Fourteen. Fourteen out of 187.
That's the real horror, David. Not the violence. The erasure. It's the only night of the year when dying in Madison doesn't count as news.
Why are you asking? Did something happen in Vilas? Are you worried?
No, I get it. You're smart to worry. Vilas usually stays quiet, but usually isn't always. Stay inside. Keep the line open. I'll check in at 23:00.
Thanks for calling, David. That's what we're here for. That's all we're here for, really — to let people know they're not the only ones who are scared.
He hung up. Good. Fewer people calling means fewer people are in immediate danger, which is a thing I'm going to tell myself so I don't think about what else it could mean.

I'm {{ELAPSED}} in and I'm already tired. It's a different kind of tired than the usual exhaustion. It's the tired you get when you've been holding your breath for sixty minutes and you know you've got ten more to go.

The Capitol breach hasn't happened yet this year, but it's coming. It always does. Some coordinated group, some political statement, some reason that makes perfect sense in the moment and zero sense on the 24th.

I used to protect that building. I used to stand on the steps and think I mattered. Now I sit in a concrete box and hope nobody I used to know is on the other side of the barricade when the glass breaks.
I'm pouring five now. I can feel the weight of the bottle changing — that's when you know the night's really started. When the drink stops being a joke and starts being a necessity.
You know what the Vance people didn't account for? That humans are animals. That you can't just flip a switch and turn off the law and expect people to play nice.
We're primates, folks. We're just primates in Wisconsin in 2169 with better weapons and worse coping mechanisms.
Caller: Robert from Williamson Street — Robert, you're on.
Robert, the neighborhood watch is a beautiful thing. I hope they hold the line. I genuinely do. But I also hope you understand what holding that line means.

It means that whatever happens outside the barricade, your neighbors stop being your neighbors and start being variables in a security equation. It means Willy Street becomes a fortress and everyone outside becomes an enemy.
I'm not criticizing — I'm explaining. That's what this night does. It makes you choose. Inside or outside. Safe or visible. And there's no third option.

You want to know if they'll hold? Probably. Willy Street's held before. But ask yourself: at what cost? Because on the 24th, someone's going to remember that you were on the inside of that barricade, and they were on the outside, and you chose your neighbors over them.
But yes, Robert. Hope they hold. I meant that.
He's gone. Good. I needed a break from my own voice for thirty seconds.

Here's something that keeps me up on the other 364 days of the year: we're not even supposed to be broadcasting this. The pirate frequency, the underground equipment, the whole operation — it's a federal crime.

Tim and his team, they spend all year setting up for one night. One night where it's technically legal to broadcast because there's no law. And everyone in government knows we're here and nobody can touch us.
It's the most honest thing the Purge has ever produced: a radio station that only exists to tell you the truth during the one time of year when telling the truth doesn't get you arrested.
Tim's probably listening to this and thinking I'm getting maudlin. I'm not. I'm getting precise. There's a difference.
The whiskey helps with precision. That's what I tell myself, anyway.
Caller: Anonymous — go ahead.
Anonymous, that's my favorite kind of caller. What do you want to tell me?
I'm sorry, you're calling from — you're calling from outside? Okay. Okay, that's brave. Or foolish. Possibly both.
I can't advise you on that. What you're doing right now, being outside at 21:00, that's not a thing I can give you permission for or warn you about. You already know what the risks are.
If you're calling to say goodbye, I don't want to hear it. If you're calling because you want someone to know you existed, I'll tell you: you existed. You're existing right now. That's enough.
Stay on the line or don't. But don't ask me to validate what you're doing, because I can't. I won't.
They hung up. They were probably just testing to see if they could reach us. Testing to see if they were real.
That's the Purge for you. It makes you question your own existence. If nobody's documenting your life, are you actually living it?
PRGE exists to document. That's our job. Tim and the engineers, they're recording everything. One night a year, the truth goes on record. The other 364 days, it disappears.

I'm six drinks in now. I've lost count a little bit, which is the first sign that I'm doing my job correctly. The whiskey should be invisible by this point. It should just be part of the fabric of the broadcast.

You can tell I'm getting looser because I'm starting to contradict myself. I said earlier that the numbers reveal something about human nature, and then I said the numbers don't mean anything because humans are just animals. Both things are true. That's the whiskey talking.
Or maybe it's the truth. Hard to tell the difference at {{CLOCK}}.

The Trump Doctrine — that was earlier. That was 2029. That's what the whole apparatus came from. 'Some people will do bad things when given permission. We should know who those people are.' That was the pitch.
And it worked. It absolutely worked. We know who those people are. They're the ones who aren't calling in right now. They're the ones doing the work.
Caller: Sarah from East Side — Sarah.
Sarah, if you need emergency services, you need to contact the volunteer crew, not me. I don't have a phone book for emergency responders because there are no emergency responders on Purge Night.
What I can do is listen. So listen, I'm listening.
Your neighbor is hurt. Okay. How badly? Can he walk? Can he make it to a medical tent if we can locate one?

Sarah, I'm going to give you the volunteer network frequency. You're going to contact them. You're going to tell them about your neighbor. They may or may not be able to help, but at least you'll have tried. That's all any of us can do.
Tim's writing down the frequency. Tim's got you. Make the call, Sarah. Right now. Don't wait.
She's gone. She's doing the right thing. Reaching out for help when help might not exist. That's survival instinct right there.

And that's why people stay on the line with me. Not because I can do anything. But because someone is listening. Someone is acknowledging that they're in trouble. Someone is saying, 'Yes, this is real, and yes, it's happening.'
That's my entire job. I'm a professional witness. I get paid in whiskey and the knowledge that I'm preventing exactly zero deaths while documenting all of them.
But I'm still here. And people are still calling. So maybe there's something to that.
I'm {{ELAPSED}} into the night and I can feel the weight of it settling in. This is the hour where the early adrenaline fades and people start to understand that there are still ten more hours to go.
Some of them will make it. Some won't. The ones who do will have a story they can't tell anyone because who would believe them? And the ones who don't will become part of the erasure.
But right now, at 21:00, they're all still here. They're all still thinking. They're all still hoping.
And I'm still pouring. This is seven. Seven is the magic number where my words start to become poetry instead of sentences. Where the drunk and the honest become the same thing.
I used to represent people who created this system. I used to believe in the necessity of it. I used to think that one night a year of chaos was worth the price of 364 days of stability.
Now I'm not sure I believe in anything except the whiskey and the people calling in. So I guess I've gotten something right, eventually.
This hour is yours, Madison. It's 21:00 and you're listening, and that's enough. That's the Purge right there.

tickerItems.text: East Side activity running 18% above 2168 trajectory at this hour. Tucker repositioning to Capitol Square for next report.
State Street Ruins: coordination confirmed. Multiple squads moving south. Willy Street barricade holding at Yahara bridge. No breach attempts yet.
Volunteer medical crews establishing secondary tent on Lake Mendota's east shore. Water temperature reported at 34 degrees. Not good numbers.
Sarah from East Side: neighbor injured. Referred to volunteer network. Tim logging location. Stand by for update.
If you're outside and unsure: Capitol area + Willy Street perimeter are most defensible positions. State Street: avoid. Avoid Beltline 22:00-02:00. Stay low.
PurgeShield panels install tomorrow. If you're still here at 7am, call 555-PANEL. 24-hour crews ready. We document what you survived.
